coredata.dc:title: Random forest algorithm-enhanced dual-emission molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensing method for rapid detection of pretilachlor in fish and water samples
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chenxi
Liao, Jingxin
Zheng, Yong
Chen, Ying
Liu, Hongsheng
Shi, Xizhi
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive and efficient fluorescence sensor based on dual-emission molecularly imprinted polymers (Dual-em-MIPs) was successfully developed using the random forest (RF) machine-learning algorithm for the rapid detection of pretilachlor. SiO2 coatings on red-emitting CdSe/ZnS quantum dots (r-SiO2@QDs) as intermediate light-emitting components are non-selective for pretilachlor, whereas molecularly imprinted layers coated with blue-emitting nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDS) are selective. Fluorescence images of the Dual-em-MIPs were acquired. The red (R), green (G), and blue (B) color values of the image were analyzed using an RF algorithm, and the classifier was trained using 103 fluorescent images for automatic analyses. Under optimized conditions, an excellent linear relationship between the sensor and pretilachlor was obtained in the range of 0.001–5.0 mg/L (R2, 0.9958). Additionally, the satisfactory recoveries of Dual-em-MIPs ranged between 92.2 % and 107.6 % for the real samples, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) under 6.5 %. The satisfactory recoveries of the RF model based on the fluorescence sensor were 84.2–108.2 % with the RSD under 6.4 %. Overall, the proposed fluorescence sensor based on Dual-em-MIPs and machine learning methods was successfully used to determine pretilachlor in the environment and in aquatic products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission fluorescence sensor
Molecularly imprinted
Machine learning
Pretilachlor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of blue-emission QDs and red-emission SiO2@QDs
Fluorescence analysis and selectivity evaluation of fluorescence sensors
Selectivity, reproducibility, and repeatability of Dual-em-MIPs
RF training and dual-mode color analysis
RF application in real sample analysis through the Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Basic Public Welfare Research Program of Zhejiang Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the Key Research and Development Program of Zhejiang Province ( 2022C02028 ), 111 Project ( B17018 ), the basic Public Welfare Research Program of Zhejiang Province ( LGC22C190001 ), Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau ( 202002N3066 , 2021Z102 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of fluorescent sensor based on Dual-em-MIPs.
TEM of r-QDs(A), r-SiO2@QDs(B), Dual-em-MIPs(C), Dual-em-NIPs(D), EDS of Dual-em-MIPs (E), Si element (F), N element (G) and O element (H).

Fluorescence spectra of Dual-em-MIPs sensor for pretilachlor detection from 0 to 5.0 mg/L as indicated by the direction of the arrows, and the radios of b-QDs: r-QDs were b:r = 1:1(A), b:r = 2:1(B), b:r = 3:1(C), b:r = 4:1(D), and b:r = 5:1(E), respectively. Combination of the fluorescence color images under a 365 nm UV lamp for a more intuitional color comparison(F).
The effect of Dual-em-MIPs amount (A) and pH (B). Pretilachlor concentration: 5.0 mg/L. Solvent: ethanol.

UV–visible absorption spectra of Dual-em-MIPs (solid black line) and Dual-em-MIP-pretilachlor*complex (A), transient visible absorption spectra of Dual-em-MIPs (B), Dual-em-MIPs-pretilachlor* complexes (C), and Dual-em-MIPs and complexes at 100 ns decay time (D) were measured with excitation pulse energy of 50 nJ. Fluorescence lifetime of the fluorescence Dual-em-MIPs (E) and Dual-em-NIPs (F) before and after adding pretilachlor (5 mg/L).

The original images taken by camera with increasing concentration of pretilachlor (A). Chromaticity diagram to indicate the range of color variation (B). The interface of running result by random forest program (C).

Fluorescence spectra of Dual-em-MIPs (A) and Dual-em-NIPs (B) with increased pretilachlor concentration. Linear calibration curves for pretilachlor determination by Dual-em-MIPs (a) and Dual-em-NIPs(b).

head.ce:title: Random forest algorithm-enhanced dual-emission molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensing method for rapid detection of pretilachlor in fish and water samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mathematics, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mathematics, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science and Technology, National University of Singapore, Science Drive 2, 117542, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Science and Technology, National University of Singapore

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science and Technology, National University of Singapore, Science Drive 2, 117542, Singapore

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Managing Biotic and Chemical Threats to the Quality and Safety of Agro-products, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Managing Biotic and Chemical Threats to the Quality and Safety of Agro-products, Ningbo University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Managing Biotic and Chemical Threats to the Quality and Safety of Agro-products, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive and efficient fluorescence sensor based on dual-emission molecularly imprinted polymers (Dual-em-MIPs) was successfully developed using the random forest (RF) machine-learning algorithm for the rapid detection of pretilachlor. SiO2 coatings on red-emitting CdSe/ZnS quantum dots (r-SiO2@QDs) as intermediate light-emitting components are non-selective for pretilachlor, whereas molecularly imprinted layers coated with blue-emitting nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDS) are selective. Fluorescence images of the Dual-em-MIPs were acquired. The red (R), green (G), and blue (B) color values of the image were analyzed using an RF algorithm, and the classifier was trained using 103 fluorescent images for automatic analyses. Under optimized conditions, an excellent linear relationship between the sensor and pretilachlor was obtained in the range of 0.001–5.0 mg/L (R2, 0.9958). Additionally, the satisfactory recoveries of Dual-em-MIPs ranged between 92.2 % and 107.6 % for the real samples, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) under 6.5 %. The satisfactory recoveries of the RF model based on the fluorescence sensor were 84.2–108.2 % with the RSD under 6.4 %. Overall, the proposed fluorescence sensor based on Dual-em-MIPs and machine learning methods was successfully used to determine pretilachlor in the environment and in aquatic products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Pretilachlor is a pre-emergent herbicide (2-chloro-2,6-diethyl-N-(2-propoxyethyl) acetanilide) that exhibits efficient weeding ability in direct-seeded rice (Chen et al., 2021). Owing to an increasing food demand to sustain the global population, the application of herbicides has increased over the years, which has improved agricultural production. However, the accumulation of pesticides in water is a concern because of their high toxicity to aquatic organisms, which poses a potential threat to the aquatic environment and human health (Elfikrie et al., 2020). Therefore, the potential risk to aquatic ecosystems increases with the accumulation of pretilachlor in water and aquatic organisms such as fish (Jiang et al., 2016). Pretilachlor can be retained in surface water, soil, and even at the harvest stage, resulting in further contamination of vegetables, fruits, rice, and drinking water, and thereby posing a threat to human health (Jiang et al., 2016; Sahoo et al., 2016, 2017).

To ensure food safety, multiple methods have been used to detect pretilachlor in the past, including high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with a UV–vis detector (HPLC-UV), fluorescence-probe sensor (Deka et al., 2019), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Liu et al., 2011). However, the lack of operational flexibility, physical/chemical stability, and economic efficiency has prompted researchers to explore more applicable and economical methods such as molecular imprinting. Molecular imprinting allows for the preparation of tailor-made synthetic biomimetic materials, called molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), which have shown promising performance in selectively recognizing targets by effectively discriminating against coexisting analogs owing to their tailor-made binding sites (Chen et al., 2016). Currently, fluorescence sensors based on molecularly imprinted polymers quantum dots (MIPs-QDs) combine the high sensitivity of MIPs and the excellent optical properties of QDs, which can be potentially used to detect trace amounts of target molecules in various environments (Wareing et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2018). Surface imprinting can be used to locate the imprinted cavities near the surface as a potential tool to reduce the mass transfer resistance and increase cavity access in MIPs-QDs (Bognar et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2019). Fluorescence sensors based on core-shell surface imprinted polymers with highly selective and sensitive responses can be used to detect pesticide residues such as carbamazepine (Elmasry et al., 2021), celastrol (Li et al., 2020), tetracycline (Wei and Chen, 2019), diethylstilbestrol/cypermethrin (Zhang et al., 2022), and propanil (Liu et al., 2020). However, only a single fluorescence peak is emitted by the above sensors, and the fluorescence intensity can be enhanced or quenched upon specific binding, which can contribute to factors such as the fluorescence background noise of the spectrophotometer and experimental operating error. Furthermore, a slight color variation in the fluorescence is difficult to detect with the naked eye. To address this challenge, dual-emission fluorescence sensors, that is, endowing the as-obtained sensors with dual-emission fluorescence peaks (bimodal fluorescence) at a single excitation wavelength, were considered owing to their self-correction function and wider fluorescence color evolution (Cai et al., 2018 ; Xu and Lu, 2015b). However, it is crucial to improve the detection efficiency and real-time determination of pesticide residues (Liu et al., 2022). Owing to the advantages of fluorescence sensors in point-of-care testing, fluorescence sensors based on intelligent colorimetric detection have been developed.

Machine learning methods for data analysis have thus far included principal component analysis (PCA) (Xu et al., 2020), linear discriminant analysis (LDA) (Lin et al., 2019), artificial neural networks (ANNs) (Movsessian et al., 2021), and RF (Xu et al., 2022). However, PCA, LDA, and ANNs have limitations in solving linearly inseparable and nonlinear classification problems, and their complexity limits their interpretability. Among the aforementioned machine learning methods, the RF algorithm is one of the most precise tools for prediction methods based on the modeling of complex interactions between predictive variables and flexible analysis of multiple statistical data, including regression, classification, and unsupervised learning (Bai et al., 2022). Because our fluorescence sensors are based on rich RGB variations (the RGB color model is an additive color model wherein red, green, and blue colors are mixed in various proportions to form a different array of colors), the RF algorithm has the advantages of better generalization ability, less overfitting, and more stable models, and the ability to assign importance scores to feature variables (Nafouanti et al., 2021). It has also been successfully used to forecast agricultural yield (Fukuda et al., 2013). Furthermore, the currently reported models were built separately for different concentrations. The lack of description of the logical relationships between samples in current models is another challenge that can cause failures in identifying samples not included in the dataset. Therefore, it is necessary to build a unified model that focuses on the logical relationships between the color and concentrations of analytes to rapidly screen and correctly identify unknown samples not included in the dataset using the RF algorithm. To the best of our knowledge, the RF model has not yet been used for the color recognition of Dual-em-MIP fluorescence sensors for rapid detection of pretilachlor.

In this study, a Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor was successfully fabricated and characterized with selectivity, signal response, and self-correction, and an RF algorithm based on Dual-em-MIPs was subsequently applied for pretilachlor detection. The increase in pretilachlor concentration resulted in quenching of the fluorescence intensity in the surface imprinted layer of blue-emitting QDs (b-QDs), whereas the fluorescence intensity of the inner r-SiO2@QDs of Dual-em-MIPs remained constant and was accompanied by a continuous variation in fluorescence color. Additionally, the RGB values of the fluorescent images of Dual-em-MIPs were analyzed using the RF algorithm and the pretilachlor concentrations were successfully predicted. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of photoelectron transfer between pretilachlor and b-QDs/Dual-em-MIPs was confirmed through transient absorption (TA) spectroscopy. The novelty of this study is that it establishes a logical link between the color of Dual-em-MIP fluorescence sensors and concentrations of pretilachlor using the RF algorithm, and proposes an instrument-free method for rapid detection of pretilachlor in aquatic environments and products.

Pretilachlor, propanil, atrazine, alachlor, and metolachlor were purchased from Shanghai Pesticide Research Institute Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Triton X-100, 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Water-soluble CdSe/ZnS QDs were purchased form BEIDA JUBANG (Beijing, China). HPLC-grade acetonitrile, magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), primary secondary amine (PSA), citric acid, urea, sodium chloride (NaCl), ethanol and ammonia solution (25 wt%) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China).

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were observed with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu, Japan), fluorescence spectrometer (FLUOROMAX-4, HoRiBA, U.S.A), and Hamamatsu C11367–11 system, respectively. fluorescence lifetime measurements were conducted using a Hamamatsu C11367–11 system. A scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Merlin, Zeiss, Germany) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEM-2100 F, JEOL, Japan) were used to analyze the morphology of polymers. The dynamic light scattering fluid dynamics diameter of nanoparticles were determined using a Zetasizer (Nano-ZS90, Malvern, UK). FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet IS50 spectrometer (Nicolet Continuum, Thermo Fisher Scientific, U.S.A). Fluorescent images were taken under an AGL-9406 UV lamp (China) with a canon digital camera (EOS 90D).

In ns-transient absorption (ns-TA) measurements, the spectra were recorded using a TA system (Transpec-NS, CIS) equipped with a nanosecond pulse laser (Opolette 355 LD, 7 ns, 20 Hz) as the pump source and a broadband laser-driven light source (EQ99X LDLS) as the probe. The pump wavelength was 450 nm. The detector output was fed to a digital oscilloscope (PicoScope 5000), which acquired the waveform and stored it for eventual data processing, and the measurement was test at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of blue-emission QDs and red-emission SiO2@QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Blue-emission N-GQDs were prepared using one-pot hydrothermal method as previously reported (Cai et al., 2018). Briefly, 2.05 g of citric acid and 1.0 g of urea were heated at 200 °C for 1 h. The resultant N-GQDs were purified via dialysis (2000 Da) for 24 h.

Red-emission SiO2@QDs nanoparticles (r-SiO2@QDs) were prepared using the modified sol-gel method (Yang et al., 2019), Followed by the addition of 500 μL (1.0 mg/mL) of CdSe/ZnS QDs, 500 μL of NH3·H2O and 37.5 mL of water successively into a 250 mL flask, and stirred for 15 min at 400 rpm. Next, 250 μL of TEOS was added dropwise under stirring. After incubation for 10 h, the mixture was added into 50.0 mL of acetone and centrifuged at 8.0 × 103 g for 10 min to obtain r-SiO2@QDs, which were washed with ethanol and centrifuged for 20 min. R-SiO2@QDs were ultrasonicated for 10 min before fabrication of dual-emission MIPs.

Dual-em-MIPs were synthesized via the modified reverse microemulsion polymerization as previously reported in the literature (Liu et al., 2020). Briefly, 143.6 μL of pretilachlor (25.0 mg/mL) and 34.2 μL of APTES were added to 8.0 mL of cyclohexane. The mixture was stirred for 2 h to obtain the pretilachlor-APTES complex, which was treated with 300.0 μL of r-SiO2@QDs (1.0 mg/mL), 50 μL of TEOS and 100 μL of NH3·H2O. The mixture was stirred for 10 h in the dark. Finally, 10.0 mL acetone was added and centrifuged at 8.0 × 103 g for 10 min. After removing supernatant, 10.0 mL deionized water was added and ultrasonicated for 10 min, followed by centrifugation at 8.0 × 103 g for 20 min to further remove the unreacted functional monomers and crosslinking agents. Acetonitrile-ethanol (8:2, v/v) was used to extract pretilachlor by shaking for 30 min. This elution process was repeated until no pretilachlor was detected by HPLC-UV. In the absence of the template, the dual emission non-imprinted polymers (Dual-em-NIPs) were synthesized following the above procedure. Dual-em-MIPs/NIPs were refrigerated in a 4 °C for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis and selectivity evaluation of fluorescence sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensities of the Dual-em-MIPs, CdSe/ZnS QDs and N-GQDs were tested with a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fluorescence measurements were tested with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and an emission wavelength range of 370–680 nm. The slit widths of excitation and emission wavelength were set at 2 nm and 5 nm, respectively. Typically, 20 μL of Dual-em-MIPs (2.0 mg/mL) in an appropriate solution and a given concentration of pretilachlor were measured after reaction for 4 min in a quartz cell (n = 3).

Snake-headed fish samples were obtained from a local market. And samples were pretreated via QuEChERS procedure described previously (Fang et al., 2021). Briefly, 5.0 g of samples were homogenized and transferred into a 50 mL centrifuge tube, followed by the addition of 8 mL of acetonitrile containing 1.0 % acetic acid. Next, 2.0 mL of the supernatant was transferred into a 15 mL centrifuge tube containing 75 mg MgSO4, 20 mg PSA and 100 mg GCB followed to vortex for 1 min. After centrifugation at 4.0 × 103 g for 15 min, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm nylon organic membrane pore prior to HPLC and fluorescence analysis.
Water sample was collected from Pearl river and were stored in glass bottles. Then the water samples were filtered through a 0.45 µm membrane to remove insoluble particles for subsequent analysis.

The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of Dual-em-MIPs and the pretilachlor concentration is shown in the graphical abstract. R-SiO2@QDs were used as an internal reference to calibrate the standard curve of Dual-em-MIPs fluorescence sensor. The calibration curve was fitted based on the relationship between the (I450/I630)0/(I450/I630) and the corresponding quencher (pretilachlor) concentrations (0.001, 0.05, 0.1, 1.0, 2.5, 5.0 mg/L) (F
0
=(I450/I630)0, F= (I450/I630), and Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F-1 =K
SV [C]), where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity before and after addition of inhibitor, respectively; K
SV is the fluorescence quenching constant; and C is the pretilachlor concentration. The accuracy and precision were evaluated based on the recoveries and RSDs, respectively.

The recyclability was evaluated by regenerating Dual-em-MIPs as described in the section of “2.3. Fabrication of Dual-em-MIPs sensor”, and their selectivity (Imprinting factor: IF=K
SV, Dual-em-MIPs /K
SV, Dual-em-NIPs ) was analyzed to evaluate the recyclability. The stability of Dual-em-MIPs was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity. The reproducibility of the Dual-em-MIPs was determined for four batches using the same procedure was evaluated by analyzing their IF values. Method validation of HPLC-UV is described in the supplementary material.

Fluorescence images of Dual-em-MIPs were obtained with digital camera in dark room under UV light with 365 nm excitation.by adding different concentration of pretilachlor. Machine learning of the captured 103 florescence images and their concentration was performed (Web: https://pan.baidu.com/s/1FPRhMtPChNl6F8Mfv8Pb-g, Password:1234). The RGB values of fluorescent images were extracted using openCV database and input into RF in the form of floating-point values. The RF model was trained to estimate each of the concentration parameters of pretilachlor and to assess the importance of fluorescence color parameters including red (R), green (G), and blue (B). The floating points of fluorescence images were input in RF model. Fluorescence images of 103 samples (Fig. 7A) were processed using Software Python (3.7.4, anaconda, on win32) numpy 1.16.5, sklearn 0.21.3, cv2 4.5.2, and the parameters of RF: n_estimators= 100. The parameters of dataset: test_size= 0.3 (which means that the proportion of this dataset for testing is 30 %), random_state= 0. "Negative mean squared error" was used as the scoring method during 10-fold crossing validation.

To perform self-correction of single-emission MIPs sensors, a double-layer structure with dual-emission MIPs-QDs was designed; the fabrication procedure is shown in Fig. 1. Because the ratio of functional monomer to template is essential for the sensitivity of Dual-em-MIPs, the molar ratio of APTES to pretilachlor was first optimized to ensure the high selectivity of Dual-em-MIPs. As shown in Fig. S3, the highest value of IF (4.26) was achieved at a ratio of 1:8, owing to the highest fluorescence quenching of the Dual-em-MIPs. In contrast, weaker fluorescence quenching of Dual-em-NIPs to pretilachlor was obtained. The results indicated that the modification of Dual-em-MIPs through APTES yielded the highest selective recognition and a satisfactory fluorescence quenching effect.

The morphological structures and size distributions of the r-SiO2@QDs and Dual-em-MIPs were determined SEM (Fig. S4), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) ( Fig. 2), respectively. As shown in Fig. S4, the r-SiO2@QDs nanoparticles (Fig. S4A) had a smooth surface and uniform size with an average diameter of approximately 49 ± 5 nm, whereas r-SiO2@QDs nanoparticles exhibited a core-shell structure with an inner core of red-emitting CdSe/ZnS QDs (Fig. 2A). Dual-em-MIPs (Fig. S4B) and Dual-em-NIPs (Fig. S4C) have a rough spherical surface with a larger particle size of approximately 95 ± 10 nm. The Dual-em-MIPs were spherical with a double-layered structure and 25 nm thickness (Fig. 2C). Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 2, the Dual-em-MIPs contained Cd, Se, Zn, and S, which are associated with red-emitting QDs (CdSe/ZnS QDs). The TEM image of the N-GQDs shown in Fig. S5 indicates that the N-GQDs were well dispersed and had similar average particle sizes of ~10 nm. The high-resolution TEM images (Fig. S5 inset) show that all N-GQDs exhibited identical well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane lattice of graphene. Additionally, common elements Si and O were observed in the Dual-em-MIPs (Fig. 2D), indicating the existence of SiO2 matrices. Specifically, N is derived from the amino group of the functional monomer, APTES. An elemental analysis of the r-SiO2@QDs is shown in Fig. S6, wherein Cd, Se, Zn, and S are present in its interior, and Si and O are present on its exterior, thereby proving that the r-QDs were successfully coated with SiO2.

The surface functional groups of the N-GQDs, r-SiO2@QDs, and Dual-em-MIPs/NIPs were further analyzed through FT-IR. As illustrated in Fig. S7, r-SiO2@QDs, and Dual-em-MIPs/NIPs have a broad absorption peak of O-H stretching vibration at 3330 cm−1. The peaks of N-GQDs and Dual-em-MIPs/NIPs at 1560 cm−1 were ascribed to secondary amide N–H bending and C–N stretching, whereas the peak of Dual-em-MIPs/NIPs at 2928 cm−1 indicates C–H stretching. The absorption peaks at 1134 cm−1 and 1062 cm−1 were ascribed to Si–O–C and Si–O–Si bonds in the polymers, respectively (Mehrzad-Samarin et al., 2017). The band detected at approximately 792 cm−1 reveals the Si–O vibrations. Dual-em-MIPs and Dual-em-NIPs share similar primary functional groups with SiO2 because there is no obvious difference in their typical bonds. These results indicate the successful coating of the imprinted layer of SiO2 on the surface of the N-GQDs and grafting of the –NH2 groups of APTES onto the r-SiO2@QDs surface.

An appropriate ratio of b-QDs and r-QDs is key for the wide color evolution of fluorescence sensors based on Dual-em-MIPs for a wider range of visual detection and collection of color datasets (Yang et al., 2019). Therefore, it is important to determine the effect of the b-QDs:r-QDs (b:r, c/c) ratio on the sensitivity of Dual-em-MIPs. As shown in Fig. 3, with an increase in the b:r ratio from 1:1–5:1, the original color of the Dual-em-MIPs changed from purple to blue with an increased concentration of b-QDs, and the color variation trend of all ratios ranged from blue to dark red (Fig. 3F). Quenching of blue-emission fluorescence after pretilachlor recognition was observed in the fluorescence spectra, with the color variation ranging from blue to dark red. As shown in Fig. 3F, the most appropriate color variation was obtained at a ratio of 4:1 (b:r, c/c). However, the low ratios of blue fluorescence, such as 1:1 and 2:1, did not contribute to the blue color in the fluorescence color evolution owing to the high concentration of red-emitting QDs that attenuated the visualization of blue. Meanwhile, the color-evolution range of the blue window was too narrow to provide a dataset of color variations with a 3:1 ratio. When the b:r ratio was 5:1, the Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor was not sensitive to pretilachlor levels at the upper limit of 7.5 × 10−3 mg/L, owing to the excessive concentration of blue-emitting N-GQDs, which prevented a wider color variation. Additionally, the color variations of Dual-em-NIPs for different b:r ratios are compared with those of Dual-em-MIPs in Fig. S8. As expected, the Dual-em-NIPs-based fluorescence sensor does not show significant color changes owing to the absence of specific binding sites on Dual-em-NIPs.

Furthermore, the Dual-em-MIPs concentrations were optimized to improve the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor. By increasing concentrations of Dual-em-MIPs, the fluorescence quenching efficiency was improved steadily, and the highest efficiency was obtained at a concentration of 2.0 mg/mL ( Fig. 4A). Hence, 2.0 mg/mL of Dual-em-MIPs was used as the optimal amount for further pretilachlor sensing.

The response time of the Dual-em-MIPs-based sensor to pretilachlor was investigated for the full combination of pretilachlor. As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence quenching efficiency (I450/I630)0/(I450/I630) increased immediately after 5.0 mg/L of pretilachlor was added and reached a plateau within 4 min, thereby indicating a rapid response of the Dual-em-MIPs to the pretilachlor.

The presence of a large number of specific groups, such as amino groups, on the surface of Dual-em-MIPs altered their selectivity based on the environmental pH. Therefore, the effect of pH in the range of 3.0–10.0 on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of Dual-em-MIPs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity of the Dual-em-MIPs was considerably stable, and a strong and specific interaction between pretilachlor and imprinted cavities of the Dual-em-MIPs was obtained under weak alkaline conditions. The optimal quenching efficiency was observed at a pH of 8.0, with an IF value of 4.69. The pKa values of APTES and pretilachlor were 10.6 and 1.41 ± 0.50, respectively. Thus, the interaction between pretilachlor and the Dual-em-MIPs occurred primarily through hydrogen bonds and ionic and van der Waals forces in the imprinted cavities, which are complementary in size and shape to pretilachlor (Mehrzad-Samarin et al., 2017; Xiao et al., 2016).

In target analyte detection, the selective interaction between Dual-em-MIPs-based sensors and analytes can affect the optical characteristics of the sensors to output specific fluorescence signals, including fluorescence brightness (quenching or enhancement), UV–vis absorption (analytes and sensors), and fluorescence lifetime through different signal transduction mechanisms (Yang et al., 2018). TA spectroscopy is a potential approach for extracting multiple excitons in QDs through ultrafast interfacial electron transfer (ET) (Huang et al., 2010). Therefore, the selective fluorescence quenching mechanism was analyzed based on UV–vis, fluorescence, TA, and fluorescence lifetime spectroscopy. In previous studies, the primary quenching mechanism of the fluorescence sensor involved photo-induced electron or Förster resonance energy transfer from N-GQDs/Dual-em-MIPs to pretilachlor. As shown in Fig. 5A, the addition of different concentrations of pretilachlor eliminated the typical UV–vis absorption peak of Dual-em-MIPs at 250 nm and 350 nm, which disappeared gradually. Compared with Dual-em-MIPs, the UV–vis absorption intensity of Dual-em-NIPs at 350 nm was not weakened by the addition of pretilachlor, thereby indicating that no selective imprinted sites were present on the non-imprinted polymers (Fig. S10A). However, there was no overlap between the emissions of Dual-em-MIPs and UV–vis absorption of pretilachlor, which indicates that the fluorescence selective quenching mechanism does not include resonance energy transfer.

Furthermore, the potential quenching mechanism of pretilachlor on Dual-em-MIPs/QDs was investigated. The TA spectra of Dual-em-MIPs and the Dual-em-MIPs-pretilachlor*-complex after 450 nm excitation are shown in Fig. 5B-5D. First, as shown in Fig. 5B, the transient spectra of the b-QDs bleaches 1 S (475 nm) and 1 P (525 nm) were excited in transitions at all delay times. The 1 S exciton bleach decayed by ~15 % within 10 ns, thereby suggesting a decay time of 10 ns. Second, when the delay time was more than 200 ns, the TA signals of the b-QDs almost disappeared, which indicates a long-lived exciton bleach in the b-QDs (without pretilachlor) (Huang et al., 2010; Xu et al., 2019). In contrast, as shown in Fig. 5C, the accelerated recovery of exciton bleach kinetics for the b-QDs-pretilachlor* complex indicates short-lived (<110 ns) 1 S (475 nm) electrons in b-QDs in the presence of adsorbed pretilachlor. Finally, as shown in Fig. 5D, the spectral signatures suggest that single exciton states decay through ultrafast interfacial ET to adsorbed pretilachlor*, as previously described (Huang et al., 2010). There was no shift in the typical TA spectra of 1S and 1P of Dual-em-NIPs after composting with pretilachlor (Fig. S10B). Therefore, a potential quenching mechanism between Dual-em-MIPs and pretilachlor could be mediated through ET. The excitons in the b-QDs of the imprinted layer can dissociate by transferring electrons to acceptors (pretilachlor). Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 5E-5 F) of the Dual-em-MIPs was 9.70 ns, which then decreased to 8.70 ns, thereby indicating that the dynamic quenching process was responsible for the decrease. The fluorescence lifetime of Dual-em-NIPs changed slightly within 0.12 ns, which indicated that the imprinted holes of Dual-em-MIPs were recombined through the template (pretilachlor) by a specific binding site and resulted in fluorescence quenching of Dual-em-MIPs/b-QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility, and repeatability of Dual-em-MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of pretilachlor and its analogs for Dual-em-MIPs (Fig. S11) were investigated. As shown in Fig. S12 the highest fluorescence quenching was achieved with the Dual-em-MIPs for pretilachlor compared with its analogs, which was attributed to the specific imprinted cavities and sites on the surface of the Dual-em-MIPs. Dual-em-NIPs exhibited no or slight fluorescence quenching responses to pretilachlor and its analogs, suggesting that no specific recognition sites were modified on their surface. Therefore, the results demonstrated that the proposed Dual-em-MIPs exhibited excellent selectivity and blue-emission quenching capability for pretilachlor. Furthermore, the specific binding of imprinting cavities to pretilachlor was the predominant factor in the fluorescence intensity quenching response.

Repeatability and reproducibility are attributes that cannot be ignored when evaluating the performance of fluorescence sensors. The fluorescence spectrum and IF of the Dual-em-MIPs were determined through six cycles of repeated elution, as shown in Fig. S13 A-B. The RSD of IF in the presence of pretilachlor (0.5 mg/L) was 3.1 %, indicating excellent stability and repeatability. The batch-to-batch reproducibility of the Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor was investigated further. An RSD of 4.18 % (n = 5) demonstrates the excellent reproducibility of the Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RF training and dual-mode color analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the detection efficiency of pretilachlor, we developed an RF machine learning method based on Dual-em-MIPs fluorescence images for real-time prediction of pretilachlor concentration. Using the optimal Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor system, the color of the fluorescent images changed from blue to dark red when the concentration of pretilachlor was increased. To train the RF model, OpenCV was used to identify the RGB value of the fluorescence images; the code is detailed in the Supplementary Material (code 1.1). By inputting RGB values (0−255) of fluorescence images in the RF algorithm, the concentration of pretilachlor was mapped, and the key features of RGB pixels were screened. The results showed that the green (G) pixel value was not linked to pretilachlor concentration.

To improve the prediction accuracy of the RF model, 70 % of the images were selected to train the RF model randomly, and the remaining 30 % of the images were used to test the training results. Additionally, the RF randomly sampled N times from N training samples by returning the way of putting back sampling to form different sample sets. Each tree trains the sample set to obtain a predicted value, and the output result is the average value of all the decision trees. Unselected cases (samples) were used for prediction and the error was evaluated. The dataset of the RF model is shown in Fig. 6, wherein the floating points of all images were generated through OpenCV, and the floating points of the training set were run by RF (Supplementary Material code 1.2). A schematic of the predicted concentration obtained using RF is shown in Fig. 6 and the graphical abstract. The training and test scores of the model were greater than 0.9 and 0.85, respectively, and the error rate was 10−5 based on 10-fold cross-validation. Crucially, a pretilachlor concentration of less than 0.015 mg/L was considered accurate in this model because most of the blue-emission fluorescence of Dual-em-MIPs was quenched under high concentrations of pretilachlor, which attenuated the ability to capture color changes. Therefore, the accurate prediction range of the concentration was 0–0.015 mg/L based on the RF model, which indicates a reliable prediction of pretilachlor concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RF application in real sample analysis through the Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The precision, linearity, accuracy, and limits of detection (LODs) of the Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor were investigated under optimal conditions. The linear formula was defined by the equation (I450/I630)0/(I450/I630) = 0.5328 C+ 1.0566 (R2, 0.9958) within the concentration range of 0.001–5.0 mg/L ( Fig. 7). Accordingly, the LOD was 0.28 μg/L (3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurements and s is the slope of the linear calibration). The LODs for snake-headed fish and river water samples were 0.67 μg/kg and 0.42 μg/L, respectively. Moreover, the Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor used to detect pretilachlor in the real samples spiked with 5.0, 50.0, and 250.0 μg/L, kg of pretilachlor. As summarized in Table S1, the recoveries of the real samples based on the Dual-em-MIPs ranged from 84.2 % to 104.2 % and their RSDs were less than 5.7 %.

The RF model was applied to model inputs to detect fluorescence images of actual samples and predict the concentration of pretilachlor; the results are presented in Table S1. Compared with the HPLC and Dual-em-MIPs, the RF model based on the fluorescence color of Dual-em-MIPs achieved high selectivity and sensitivity. The recoveries were between 84.2 % and 108.2 %, and the RSDs were under 6.4 %. Thus, the RF model developed using the machine learning method represents a novel strategy for detecting pretilachlor in the field. Meanwhile, the recoveries of fish and river samples by HPLC (n = 3) listed in Table S1 indicate that the recoveries of the real samples were between 95.5 % and 111.8 %, and the highest RSD was 5.7 %. Therefore, the Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor and RF model facilitated the accurate and precise detection of pretilachlor in fish and river water samples.

To highlight the performance and applications of the Dual-em-MIPs and RF model based on the Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor, the advantages and limitations were summarized through comparisons with various types of pretilachlor detection methods (Table S2). First, HPLC and matrix solid phase dispersion methods based on HPLC are precise and accurate for the pretilachlor detection, but they are limited owing to their high cost and tedious operation. Rapid detection methods, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), electrochemiluminescence (ECL), and fluorescence-probe sensors, can easily detect pretilachlor in real samples; however, they also have some disadvantages that limit their application. For example, poor reproducibility and false positives occur in the ELISA detection process, and the electrical signals of the ECL can be easily affected. In this study, an instrument-free, sensitive, visual, and self-correcting functional sensing platform of RF based on the Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensing method was developed to enhance the sensitivity and selectivity of pretilachlor detection. The high selectivity of Dual-em-MIPs resulted in a higher sensitivity (LODs of 0.67 μg/kg and 0.42 μg/L for fish and river water samples, respectively) and wider linearity range (0.001–5.0 mg/L) compared with previous references. Meanwhile, the lower LOD (0.05 μg/L) and satisfactory recovery (95.5–111.8 %) of RF model that were obtained are listed in Table S2. Thus, the results demonstrated the capability of the proposed Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor and RF model for detecting pretilachlor in real samples. However, it should be noted that the RF model based on Dual-em-MIPs has some limitations. For example, it requires a large and appropriate dataset for training, resulting in some complexity in the model design.

An innovative method for the quantitative detection of pretilachlor in complex samples was developed by adopting Dual-em-MIPs combined with machine learning. This Dual-em-MIPs identified pretilachlor with a turn-off outer imprinted layer of blue-emission fluorescence and turn-on of inner red-emission fluorescence, thereby generating a remarkably profuse concentration-dependent color evolution of the blue-purple-red window. Furthermore, the fluorescence of Dual-em-MIPs after the addition of pretilachlor was photographed, and the colors in the image were analyzed using the RF algorithm. The prepared Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor exhibited a good linear relationship between 0.001 and 5 mg/L (R2 = 0.9958), and obtained LODs of 0.67 μg/kg and 0.42 μg/L for fish and river water samples, respectively. The recoveries of RF and Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensors in real samples were between 92.2 % and 107.6 % and 84.2–108.2 %, respectively. In summary, the Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor and RF algorithm exhibited excellent sensitivity, desirable selectivity, reproducibility, and repeatability, thereby offering a potential method for application in pretilachlor detection in the environment and aquatic products. Additionally, establishing an original and universal RF model will assist the ratiometric fluorescence assay of pretilachlor.

Pretilachlor [2-chloro-N-(2,6-diethylphenyl)-N-(2-propoxyethyl) acetamide] is highly toxic and hazardous to aquatic organisms. Therefore, its accumulation in aquatic environments and its concentrations in aquatic organisms, such as fish and shrimp, pose a potential risk to aquatic ecosystems. We present a facile method to prepare a Dual-em-MIPs-based fluorescence sensor to enable visual detection of pretilachlor and an RF machine learning method for real-time prediction of pretilachlor concentration. The fluorescence sensor and RF were successfully applied for detecting pretilachlor in aquatic environments and products with excellent sensitivity, selectivity, reproducibility, and repeatability.

On behalf of my co-authors, I certify that all authors have seen and approved the final version of the manuscript being submitted. We warrant that the article is the original work, hasn't received prior publication and isn't under consideration for publication elsewhere.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Key Research and Development Program of Zhejiang Province (2022C02028), 111 Project (B17018), the basic Public Welfare Research Program of Zhejiang Province (LGC22C190001), Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (202002N3066, 2021Z102).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2022.129591.

Chem. 91, 6561–6568. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.9b00082.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Yang et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategies of molecular imprinting-based fluorescence sensors for chemical and biological analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2022

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly selective fluorescence nanosensor based on the dual-function molecularly imprinted layer coated quantum dots for the sensitive detection of diethylstilbestrol/cypermethrin in fish and seawater

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, R., Li, X., Sun, A., Song, S., Shi, X., 2022. A highly selective fluorescence nanosensor based on the dual-function molecularly imprinted layer coated quantum dots for the sensitive detection of diethylstilbestrol/cypermethrin in fish and seawater.
